claim: The ingots were hot rolled from 20 to 2 mm at 1000 °C and water quenched; then solution treated at 1000 °C for 30 min and water quenched; subsequently aged at 550 °C for 10 h or 30 h, followed by water quenching. Solution-treated steels are designated M1-SS and M2-SS; aged samples are M1-30, M2-10, and M2-30.
evidence_text: The ingots were hot rolled from 20 to 2 mm at 1000 ◦C followed by water quenching. Subsequently, the samples were solution-processed at 1000 ◦C for 30 min and water quenched, followed by aging at $5 5 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ for 10h and 30h, followed again by water quenching. The two steels after solution treatment were named M1-SS and M2-SS. After aging treatment, they were named M1-30, M2-10 and M2-30.

claim: M1 steel exhibits equiaxed grains with no significant change after aging; M1-30 maintains an average grain size of 45.3 ± 9.3 μm. M2 steel has refined equiaxed grains: M2-SS average 32.3 ± 10.4 μm, M2-30 average 35.8 ± 12.7 μm (Table 2). EBSD reveals no significant texture; M2-SS shows a bimodal grain distribution, and M2-30 becomes unimodal after aging (Fig. 2).
evidence_text: Fig. 1 (a) and (b) show the solution-treated and 30 h aged conditions of M1 steel respectively, both exhibiting equiaxed grain morphology. After aging treatment, the grain size of M1 specimen shows no significant change, maintaining an average of 45.3 ± 9.3 μm. Fig. 1 (c) and (d) display the microstructures of M2 steel in solution-treated and aged 30 h conditions. The M2 steel also demonstrates equiaxed grains with an average size of approximately 32.3 ± 10.4 μm. ... The grain sizes of M1 and M2 are listed in Table 2.

claim: In solution-treated M1-SS, no precipitates are observed within the austenite matrix. In solution-treated M2-SS, intragranular (Ti,V)C carbides are present with an average size of 35.8 ± 20.1 nm (Fig. 3).
evidence_text: Fig. 3 (a) presents the austenite matrix of M1 after solution treatment. Since Cr and Mo do not precipitate at the solution temperature, no precipitates are observed within the austenite matrix. In contrast, Fig. 3 (b) displays the microstructure of M2-SS, which contains precipitates in addition to the matrix. ... The intragranular (Ti, V)C carbides exhibit an average size of 35.8 ± 20.1 nm.

claim: In M2 steel aged 10 h, κ-carbides preferentially precipitate at the edges of (Ti,V)C carbides, with almost no κ-carbides in the matrix. After 30 h aging, κ-carbides increase in number density and size but remain heterogeneously distributed at (Ti,V)C edges (Fig. 4d-j).
evidence_text: Fig. 4(d–f) shows κ-carbides and (Ti, V)C carbides in M2 steel aged for 10 h. The SAED image shows the presence of (Ti, V) C carbides and κ-carbides. Notably, although the precipitation of κ-carbide is limited to aging time, it preferentially precipitates at the edge of (Ti, V)C carbides, while almost no κ-carbides precipitate in the matrix. Fig. 4(g–j) ... revealing increased number density and larger size compared to the 10 h aged sample, with the distribution remaining heterogeneous.

claim: HRTEM and FFT analysis of M2-30 (Fig. 6) reveal an intergrowth of κ-carbides and (Ti,V)C carbides with the orientation relationship (002)_(Ti,V)C || (001)_κ.
evidence_text: Fig. 6 (b) shows the high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) image of the region outlined in the purple box in Fig. 6 (a), demonstrating a typical intergrowth of κ-carbide and (Ti, V)C carbide. ... The Fast Fourier Transform (FFT) analysis confirms that the (002) plane of (Ti, V)C is parallel to the (001) plane of κ-carbide, denoted as $( 0 0 2 ) _ { \mathrm { ( T i } } ,$ V)C || (001)κ.

claim: HRTEM and FFT analysis (Fig. 6) of M2-30 reveal intergrowth of κ-carbide and (Ti,V)C carbide, with κ-carbides linearly aligned along (Ti,V)C edges and orientation relationship (002)(Ti,V)C || (001)κ.
evidence_text: Fig. 6 (b) shows the high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) image … demonstrating a typical intergrowth of κ-carbide and (Ti, V)C carbide. … these results indicate a linear alignment of κ-carbides … Fast Fourier Transform (FFT) analysis confirms that the (002) plane of (Ti, V)C is parallel to the (001) plane of κ-carbide, denoted as (002)(Ti,V)C || (001)κ.

claim: Tensile tests (Fig. 7) show that aged M1 (M1-30) has yield strength 860 MPa and elongation reduced to 40%, while aged M2 (M2-30) yields 884 MPa with slight elongation loss; solution-treated M2 yield strength is ~200 MPa higher than M1; work-hardening rate of aged M2 remains comparable to its solution-treated state, unlike M1 which shows significant reduction.
evidence_text: The yield strength of solution-treated M1 steel was approximately 430 MPa, which increased to 860 MPa after aging … accompanied by a reduction in elongation to 40 %. In comparison, solution-treated M2 steel exhibited a ~200 MPa higher yield strength than M1. After aging, M2 reached a yield strength of 884 MPa with only a slight decrease in elongation. The work-hardening rate curves revealed that aged M1 steel showed a significant reduction … whereas aged M2 steel maintained a work-hardening rate comparable to its solution-treated state.

claim: According to Table 3, in M1-30 κ-carbides have volume fraction 7.4 ± 3.4% and mean radius 10.8 ± 6.2 nm; in M2-30 κ-carbides volume fraction is 3.2 ± 2.8% and mean radius 9.7 ± 8.5 nm, while (Ti,V)C volume fraction is 2.3 ± 1.7% and mean radius 37.1 ± 22.6 nm.
evidence_text: Table 3 Volume fraction and mean radius of (Ti, V)C and κ: M1-30 κ volume fraction 7.4 ± 3.4%, mean radius 10.8 ± 6.2 nm; M2-SS (Ti,V)C 2.4 ± 1.4%, 35.8 ± 20.1 nm; M2-30 (Ti,V)C 2.3 ± 1.7%, 37.1 ± 22.6 nm, κ 3.2 ± 2.8%, 9.7 ± 8.5 nm.

claim: In Fe-Mn-Al-C steels, carbon is the predominant factor governing κ-carbide precipitation sites because Mn and Al concentrations are identical in M1 and M2 yet precipitation behaviors differ; high-temperature (Ti,V)C precipitates modulate matrix carbon content prior to aging to achieve heterogeneous nucleation.
evidence_text: Carbon is identified as the predominant factor governing κ-carbide precipitation. … This necessitates regulation of the matrix carbon content prior to aging treatment. Consequently, the formation of high-temperature precipitates becomes essential. The addition of Ti and V promotes the precipitation of (Ti, V)C carbides before aging, which effectively modulates the residual carbon concentration in the matrix.

claim: A schematic (Fig. 9) illustrates that before aging, preexisting (Ti,V)C carbides in M2 are surrounded by carbon-enriched zones; upon aging at 550 °C, κ-carbides preferentially precipitate along the edges of (Ti,V)C utilizing these carbon-rich regions.
evidence_text: Fig. 9 presents a schematic illustration … Before aging treatment, preexisting (Ti, V)C carbides … are distributed in the matrix, accompanied by surrounding carbon-enriched zones … Upon aging at 550 °C, κ-carbides … preferentially precipitated along the edge of (Ti, V)C carbides, utilizing these carbon-rich regions as precipitation sites.

claim: Quantified yield strength contributions (Fig. 10) show that M1-30 derives 430 MPa from κ shearing (σS), with σ0=350 MPa, σG=90 MPa; M2-30 derives 220 MPa from κ shearing, 120 MPa from Orowan bypass of (Ti,V)C, σ0=410 MPa, σG=100 MPa, resulting in measured yield strengths 860 MPa and 880 MPa respectively.
evidence_text: Fig. 10: contributions … M1-30 measured 860 MPa, σ0=350 MPa, σG=90 MPa, σS=430 MPa; M2-30 measured 880 MPa, σ0=410 MPa, σG=100 MPa, σOr=120 MPa, σS=220 MPa.

claim: The stacking-fault energy (SFE) of M1 and M2 is approximately 70 mJ/m² and 78 mJ/m², respectively, which indicates that the deformation mode is dislocation slip.
evidence_text: Through calculations, the SFE of M1 and M2 is approximately 70 mJ/m² and 78 mJ/m², which indicates that the deformation mode of the material is the dislocation slip.

claim: According to Crussard–Jaoul analysis in Fig. 11, M1 solid-solution shows three work-hardening stages while M1 aged shows two stages, and the work-hardening index curves of M2 before and after aging almost coincide, indicating that uniform κ-carbide precipitation adversely affects work-hardening ability.
evidence_text: In Fig. 11, the work hardening of solid-solution samples without Ti and V is divided into three stages. After aging, the work hardening is divided into two stages. ... Compared to M1, the work-hardening index curves of M2 before and after aging almost coincide, which indicates that the uniform precipitation of κ-carbides affects the work-hardening ability of low-density steel.

claim: TEM images of deformed specimens (Fig. 12a,b) show that both M1 and M2 deform by planar dislocation slip, but M2 exhibits significantly smaller slip-band spacing than M1, indicating more severe deformation and refined dislocation configuration.
evidence_text: The TEM images of the uniformly deformed region after tensile testing, as shown in Fig. 12(a) and (b), reveal that the deformation mode of both M1 and M2 is the plane slip of dislocation... it can be seen that the slip-band spacing of M2 steel is significantly smaller than that of M1 steel

claim: κ-carbides are shearable and highly coherent, leading to glide plane softening after being cut by dislocations, whereas (Ti,V)C carbides are non-shearable, hinder dislocations, and create regions of high dislocation density that increase flow stress (Fig. 12c).
evidence_text: Compared to (Ti, V)C carbides, κ-carbides have a much higher coherence with the matrix. ... This highly coherent κ-carbides can destroy the coherence relationship after being cut by dislocation. This in turn softens the glide plane. ... the (Ti, V)C carbides can hinder the dislocations as shown in Fig. 12 (c). Moreover, a region with higher dislocation density is formed around it, which increases the flow stress.

claim: The heterogeneous distribution of κ-carbides combined with non-shearable (Ti,V)C particles prevents glide plane softening and maintains the work-hardening rate in M2-30 nearly unchanged.
evidence_text: And this heterogeneous distribution of κ-carbides combined with (Ti, V)C carbides will not soften the glide plane... These two reasons result in the work-hardening rate remaining almost unchanged.

claim: Ti/V-modified steel M2 shows ~200 MPa higher yield strength than Ti/V-free steel after solution treatment due to (Ti,V)C precipitation, and reaches 884 MPa after aging, comparable to 860 MPa in M1-30, with dual strengthening from Orowan bypassing and coherency strengthening.
evidence_text: The yield strength of Ti/V-modified steel increases significantly after solution treatment (by ~200 MPa) due to (Ti, V)C precipitation. After aging, the yield strength further rises to 884 MPa, comparable to Ti/V-free steel (860 MPa), despite the reduced κ-carbide volume fraction. The dual strengthening effect arises from Orowan bypassing of non-shearable (Ti, V)C and coherency strengthening of shearable κ-carbides.

claim: The yield strength of Ti/V-modified steel increases significantly after solution treatment by ~200 MPa due to (Ti,V)C precipitation, and after aging rises to 884 MPa, comparable to 860 MPa for Ti/V-free steel, despite reduced κ-carbide volume fraction; dual strengthening arises from Orowan bypassing of non-shearable (Ti,V)C and coherency strengthening of shearable κ-carbides.
evidence_text: The yield strength of Ti/V-modified steel increases significantly after solution treatment (by ~200 MPa) due to (Ti, V)C precipitation. After aging, the yield strength further rises to 884 MPa, comparable to Ti/V-free steel (860 MPa), despite the reduced κ-carbide volume fraction. The dual strengthening effect arises from Orowan bypassing of non-shearable (Ti, V)C and coherency strengthening of shearable κ-carbides.
